title: The Day-Dream
content:
PROLOGUE

O Lady Flora, let me speak:
A pleasant hour has passed away
While, dreaming on your damask cheek,
The dewy sister-eyelids lay.
As by the lattice you reclined,
I went throï¿½ many wayward moods
To see you dreamingï¿½and, behind,
A summer crisp with shining woods.
And I too dreamï¿½d, until at last
Across my fancy, brooding warm,
The reflex of a legend past,
And loosely settled into form.
And would you have the thought I had,
And see the vision that I saw,
Then take the broidery-frame, and add
A crimson to the quaint Macaw,
And I will tell it. Turn your face,
Nor look with that too-earnest eyeï¿½
The rhymes are dazzled from their place
And orderï¿½d words asunder fly.



THE SLEEPING PALACE

I.

The varying year with blade and sheaf
Clothes and reclothes the happy plains,
Here rests the sap within the leaf,
Here stays the blood along the veins.
Faint shadows, vapours lightly curlï¿½d,
Faint murmurs from the meadows come,
Like hints and echoes of the world
To spirits folded in the womb.

II.

Soft lustre bathes the range of urns
On every slanting terrace-lawn.
The fountain to his place returns
Deep in the garden lake withdrawn.
Here droops the banner on the tower,
On the hall-hearths the festal fires,
The peacock in his laurel bower,
The parrot in his gilded wires.

III.

Roof-haunting martins warm their eggs:
In these, in those the life is stayï¿½d.
The mantles from the golden pegs
Droop sleepily: no sound is made,
Not even of a gnat that sings.
More like a picture seemeth all
Than those old portraits of old kings,
That watch the sleepers from the wall.

IV.

Here sits the Butler with a flask
Between his knees, half-drainï¿½d; and there
The wrinkled steward at his task,
The maid-of-honour blooming fair;
The page has caught her hand in his:
Her lips are severï¿½d as to speak:
His own are pouted to a kiss:
The blush is fixï¿½d upon her cheek.

V.

Till all the hundred summers pass,
The beams, that throï¿½ the Oriel shine,
Make prisms in every carven glass,
And beaker brimmï¿½d with noble wine.
Each baron at the banquet sleeps,
Grave faces gatherï¿½d in a ring.
His state the king reposing keeps.
He must have been a jovial king.

VI.

All round a hedge upshoots, and shows
At distance like a little wood;
Thorns, ivies, woodbine, mistletoes,
And grapes with bunches red as blood;
All creeping plants, a wall of green
Close-matted, bur and brake and briar,
And glimpsing over these, just seen,
High up, the topmost palace spire.

VII.

When will the hundred summers die,
And thought and time be born again,
And newer knowledge, drawing nigh,
Bring truth that sways the soul of men?
Here all things in their place remain,
As all were orderï¿½d, ages since.
Come, Care and Pleasure, Hope and Pain,
And bring the fated fairy Prince.



THE SLEEPING BEAUTY

I.

Year after year unto her feet,
She lying on her couch alone,
Across the purple coverlet,
The maidenï¿½s jet-black hair has grown,
On either side her tranced form
Forth streaming from a braid of pearl:
The slumbrous light is rich and warm,
And moves not on the rounded curl.

II.

The silk star-broiderï¿½d coverlid
Unto her limbs itself doth mould
Languidly ever; and, amid
Her full black ringlets downward rollï¿½d,
Glows forth each softly-shadowï¿½d arm
With bracelets of the diamond bright:
Her constant beauty doth inform
Stillness with love, and day with light.

III.

She sleeps: her breathings are not heard
In palace chambers far apart.
The fragrant tresses are not stirrï¿½d
That lie upon her charmed heart.
She sleeps: on either hand upswells
The gold-fringed pillow lightly prest:
She sleeps, nor dreams, but ever dwells
A perfect form in perfect rest.



THE ARRIVAL

I.

All precious things, discoverï¿½d late,
To those that seek them issue forth;
For love in sequel works with fate,
And draws the veil from hidden worth.
He travels far from other skiesï¿½
His mantle glitters on the rocksï¿½
A fairy Prince, with joyful eyes,
And lighter-footed than the fox.

II.

The bodies and the bones of those
That strove in other days to pass,
Are witherï¿½d in the thorny close,
Or scatterï¿½d blanching on the grass.
He gazes on the silent dead:
ï¿½They perish'd in their daring deeds.ï¿½
This proverb flashes throï¿½ his head,
ï¿½The many fail: the one succeeds.ï¿½

III.

He comes, scarce knowing what he seeks:
He breaks the hedge: he enters there:
The colour flies into his cheeks:
He trusts to light on something fair;
For all his life the charm did talk
About his path, and hover near
With words of promise in his walk,
And whisperï¿½d voices at his ear.

IV.

More close and close his footsteps wind:
The Magic Music in his heart
Beats quick and quicker, till he find
The quiet chamber far apart.
His spirit flutters like a lark,
He stoopsï¿½to kiss herï¿½on his knee.
ï¿½Love, if thy tresses be so dark,
How dark those hidden eyes must be!ï¿½



THE REVIVAL

I.

A touch, a kiss! the charm was snapt.
There rose a noise of striking clocks,
And feet that ran, and doors that clapt,
And barking dogs, and crowing cocks;
A fuller light illumined all,
A breeze throï¿½ all the garden swept,
A sudden hubbub shook the hall,
And sixty feet the fountain leapt.

II.

The hedge broke in, the banner blew,
The butler drank, the steward scrawlï¿½d,
The fire shot up, the martin flew,
The parrot screamï¿½d, the peacock squallï¿½d,
The maid and page renewï¿½d their strife,
The palace bangï¿½d, and buzzï¿½d and clackt,
And all the long-pent stream of life
Dashï¿½d downward in a cataract.

III.

And last with these the king awoke,
And in his chair himself uprearï¿½d,
And yawnï¿½d, and rubbï¿½d his face, and spoke,
ï¿½By holy rood, a royal beard!
How say you? we have slept, my lords.
My beard has grown into my lap.ï¿½
The barons swore, with many words,
ï¿½Twas but an after-dinnerï¿½s nap.

IV.

ï¿½Pardy,ï¿½ retnrnï¿½d the king, ï¿½but still
My joints are somewhat stiff or so.
My lord, and shall we pass the bill
I mentionï¿½d half an hour ago?ï¿½
The chancellor, sedate and vain,
In courteous words returnï¿½d reply:
But dallied with his golden chain,
And, smiling, put the question by.



THE DEPARTURE

I.

And on her loverï¿½s arm she leant,
And round her waist she felt it fold,
And far across the hills they went
In that new world which is the old:
Across the hills, and far away
Beyond their utmost purple rim,
And deep into the dying day
The happy princess followï¿½d him.

II.

ï¿½Iï¿½d sleep another hundred years,
O love, for such another kiss;ï¿½
ï¿½O wake for ever, love,ï¿½ she hears,
ï¿½O love, ï¿½twas such as this and this.ï¿½
And oï¿½er them many a sliding star,
And many a merry wind was borne,
And, streamï¿½d throï¿½ many a golden bar,
The twilight melted into morn.

III.

ï¿½O eyes long laid in happy sleep!ï¿½
ï¿½O happy sleep, that lightly fled!ï¿½
ï¿½O happy kiss, that woke thy sleep!ï¿½
ï¿½O love, thy kiss would wake the dead!ï¿½
And oï¿½er them many a flowing range
Of vapour buoyï¿½d the crescent-bark,
And, rapt throï¿½ many a rosy change,
The twilight died into the dark.

IV.

ï¿½A hundred summers! can it be?
And whither goest thou, tell me where?ï¿½
ï¿½O seek my fatherï¿½s court with me,
For there are greater wonders there.ï¿½
And oï¿½er the hills, and far away
Beyond their utmost purple rim,
Beyond the night, across the day,
Throï¿½ all the world she followï¿½d him.



MORAL

I.

So, Lady Flora, take my lay,
And if you find no moral there,
Go, look in any glass and say,
What moral is in being fair.
Oh, to what uses shall we put
The wildweed-flower that simply blows?
And is there any moral shut
Within the bosom of the rose?

II.

But any man that walks the mead,
In bud or blade, or bloom, may find,
According as his humours lead,
A meaning suited to his mind.
And liberal applications lie
In Art like Nature, dearest friend;
So ï¿½twere to cramp its use, if I
Should hook it to some useful end.



 Lï¿½ENVOI

I.

You shake your head. A random string
Your finer female sense offends.
Wellï¿½were it not a pleasant thing
To fall asleep with all oneï¿½s friends;
To pass with all our social ties
To silence from the paths of men;
And every hundred years to rise
And learn the world, and sleep again;
To sleep throï¿½ terms of mighty wars,
And wake on science grown to more,
On secrets of the brain, the stars,
As wild as aught of fairy lore;
And all that else the years will show,
The Poet-forms of stronger hours,
The vast Republics that may grow,
The Federations and the Powers;
Titanic forces taking birth
In divers seasons, divers climes;
For we are Ancients of the earth,
And in the morning of the times.

II.

So sleeping, so aroused from sleep
Throï¿½ sunny decades new and strange,
Or gay quinquenniads would we reap
The flower and quintessence of change.

III.

Ah, yet would Iï¿½and would I might!
So much your eyes my fancy takeï¿½
Be still the first to leap to light
That I might kiss those eyes awake!
For, am I right, or am I wrong,
To choose your own you did not care;
Youï¿½d have my moral from the song,
And I will take my pleasure there:
And, am I right or am I wrong,
My fancy, ranging throï¿½ and throï¿½,
To search a meaning for the song,
Perforce will still revert to you;
Nor finds a closer truth than this
All-graceful head, so richly curlï¿½d,
And evermore a costly kiss
The prelude to some brighter world.

IV.

For since the time when Adam first
Embraced his Eve in happy hour,
And every bird of Eden burst
In carol, every bud to flower,
What eyes, like thine, have wakenï¿½d hopes,
What lips, like thine, so sweetly joinï¿½d?
Where on the double rosebud droops
The fulness of the pensive mind;
Which all too dearly self-involved,
Yet sleeps a dreamless sleep to me;
A sleep by kisses undissolved,
That lets thee neither hear nor see:
But break it. In the name of wife,
And in the rights that name may give,
Are claspï¿½d the moral of thy life,
And that for which I care to live.



EPILOGUE

So, Lady Flora, take my lay,
And, if you find a meaning there,
O whisper to your glass, and say,
ï¿½What wonder, if he thinks me fair?ï¿½
What wonder I was all unwise,
To shape the song for your delight
Like long-tailï¿½d birds of Paradise
That float throï¿½ Heaven, and cannot light?
Or old-world trains, upheld at court
By Cupid-boys of blooming hueï¿½
But take itï¿½earnest wed with sport,
And either sacred unto you.
meaning:
Alfred Lord Tennyson's contribution to classic is further solidified by the brilliance found in "The Day-Dream"...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
